source: VS Partners / Financial Times Global Seattle Symposium — Global technology investment: hype, hope, reality
summary: Breaking up big tech would likely be bullish, not bearish — conglomerate discount embedded in mega-caps
summary_extended: Baker argues that a forced breakup of large technology companies would likely be bullish for shareholders, not bearish, because these mega-caps trade at a conglomerate discount relative to the sum of their parts. He draws on historical precedent — the breakups of AT&T and Standard Oil — as evidence that forced separations have historically created enormous shareholder value rather than destroyed it. Baker frames this as a deliberately contrarian view, noting that most investors treat antitrust risk as a clear negative scenario, when in fact the opposite may be true. He does not specify a particular company as the primary target, but the implication is that the largest platform companies (e.g., Alphabet, Meta, Amazon, Apple) are the relevant universe. The investment implication is that antitrust risk may be mispriced as a negative catalyst when it could actually serve as a value-unlocking event.
detail: Baker argues that historical breakups (AT&T, Standard Oil) created immense value. He believes there is a conglomerate discount embedded in large tech companies, and a breakup would unlock value. He frames this as a contrarian view — most investors fear antitrust as a negative scenario.
confidence_evidence: Baker says 'I actually think the hard thing is that's probably bullish' — stated as a personal view but acknowledged as counterintuitive. Not a core portfolio thesis, more of a regulatory framework observation.
quote_fragment: i don't think a breakup would be a kind of a negative scenario
